title: Bitcoin as the Ultimate Protocol for Storing Economic Energy

source.original: You Have 10 Years to Join the AI Economy | Michael Saylor

body_html:
Throughout history, civilizations have risen and fallen based on the quality of their foundational protocols — language, mathematics, science, and money. The most powerful protocol displaces the weakest. Nations on the gold standard displaced those on glass beads because gold couldn't be manufactured at will. Bronze displaced wood, steel displaced bronze, and explosives displaced masonry. Bitcoin represents the next leap: the highest-integrity protocol humanity has discovered for storing and moving economic energy through time and space.

The analogy to Arabic numerals is instructive. Arabic numerals weren't the first mathematical system — they were simply the best. When they arrived, they obliterated Greek, Babylonian, and Roman numeral systems entirely because they were more stable, rational, and efficient. Bitcoin has done the same in global capital markets: it provides a universally agreed-upon, peer-to-peer system for trading value across thousands of exchanges with thermodynamic soundness and cryptographic integrity.

What makes Bitcoin superior to prior stores of value is its unique combination of properties. Gold, the previous champion of portable capital, proved too slow and heavy for the 20th-century economy — you cannot move $10 billion in gold bullion across an ocean fourteen times a day. Real estate cannot run or hide; it gets triple-taxed and is trivially seized by any government that chooses to do so. Art and diamonds require authentication, physical custody, and counterparty trust. Bitcoin eliminates all of these friction points.

The property rights embedded in Bitcoin ownership are qualitatively different from owning shares in a REIT or a limited partnership in a painting. With Bitcoin, even $22 worth gives you the same sovereign property rights as a whale holding billions. There is no gatekeeper, no regulator blocking a Saturday afternoon transaction, and no bank that must approve the transfer. This is what makes it the hardest asset to tax, steal, or impair — and why an armed society, in the financial sense, tends to be a polite one.
